fact: A ship (the Grand Saint-Antoine) docked in Marseille carrying plague, and merchants pressured officials to relax quarantine to protect their cargo of silk and cotton
Dockworkers were among the first to die, with deaths beginning within weeks of the ship's arrival in late May 1720
source: Plague and the City, ed. Lukas Engelmann, John Henderson, Christos Lynteris (Routledge, 2018); also widely documented in contemporary accounts

fact: Authorities erected walls (the Mur de la Peste) and posted armed guards to quarantine infected districts
Bodies accumulated faster than they could be buried, leading to mass graves and the use of convicts (galériens) to clear corpses from the streets
source: Marseille's municipal archives and Chevalier Roze's documented efforts; summarized in Wikipedia — Great Plague of Marseille

fact: Approximately 50,000 people died in Marseille out of a population of roughly 100,000 — about half the city
source: Wikipedia — Great Plague of Marseille (citing contemporary and modern estimates of 50,000 deaths in the city proper, up to 100,000 in the region)
